Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ where $$a_{n} = \frac{2}{n ( n + 1 )}$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ where $$a_{n} = \frac{1}{n ( n + 1 \text{/} 2 )}$$
Answer: Converges by comparison with $$1 \text{/} n^{2} .$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2 ( n + 1 )}$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2 ( n - 1 )}$$
Answer: The sum is one half times a harmonic series, which diverges.

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{( n \text{ln} n )^{2}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{n \text{!}}{( n + 2 ) \text{!}}$$
Answer: $$a_{n} = 1 \text{/} ( n + 1 ) ( n + 2 ) < 1 \text{/} n^{2} .$$ Converges by comparison with p -series, $$p = 2 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n \text{!}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{\text{sin} ( 1 \text{/} n )}{n^{2}}$$
Answer: $$\text{sin} ( 1 \text{/} n ) \le 1 \text{/} n ,$$ so converges by comparison with p -series, $$p = 2 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{\text{sin}^{2} n}{n^{2}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{\text{sin} ( 1 \text{/} n )}{( \sqrt{n} )^{3}}$$
Answer: $$\text{sin} ( 1 \text{/} n ) \le 1 ,$$ so converges by comparison with p -series, $$p = 3 \text{/} 2 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{n^{1.2} - 1}{n^{2.3} + 1}$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{\sqrt{n + 1} - \sqrt{n}}{n}$$
Answer: Since $$\sqrt{n + 1} - \sqrt{n} = 1 \text{/} ( \sqrt{n + 1 / 2} + \sqrt{n} ) \le 2 \text{/} \sqrt{n} ,$$ series converges by comparison with p -series for $$p = 1.5.$$

Problem Statement: Use the comparison test to determine whether the following series converge.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{\sqrt[4]{n}}{\sqrt[3]{n^{4} + n^{2}}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} ( \frac{\text{ln} n}{n} )^{2}$$
Answer: Converges by limit comparison with p -series for $$p > 1 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} ( \frac{\text{ln} n}{n^{0.6}} )^{2}$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{\text{ln} ( 1 + \frac{1}{n} )}{n}$$
Answer: Converges by limit comparison with p -series, $$p = 2 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \text{ln} ( 1 + \frac{1}{n^{2}} )$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{4^{n} - 3^{n}}$$
Answer: Converges by limit comparison with $$4^{\text{unicode[:-]} n} .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{2} - n \text{sin} n}$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^{( 1.1 ) n} - 3^{n}}$$
Answer: Converges by limit comparison with $$1 \text{/} e^{1.1 n} .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{e^{( 1.01 ) n} - 3^{n}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n^{1 + 1 \text{/} n}}$$
Answer: Diverges by limit comparison with harmonic series.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^{1 + 1 \text{/} n} n^{1 + 1 \text{/} n}}$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} ( \frac{1}{n} - \text{sin} ( \frac{1}{n} ) )$$
Answer: Converges by limit comparison with p -series, $$p = 3 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} ( 1 - \text{cos} ( \frac{1}{n} ) )$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n} ( \frac{\pi}{2} - \text{tan}^{-1} n )$$
Answer: Converges by limit comparison with p -series, $$p = 3 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} ( 1 - \frac{1}{n} )^{n . n}$$ ( Hint: $$( 1 - \frac{1}{n} )^{n} \vec{} 1 \text{/} e . )$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} ( 1 - e^{-1 \text{/} n} )$$ ( Hint: $$1 \text{/} e \approx ( 1 - 1 \text{/} n )^{n} ,$$ so $$1 - e^{-1 \text{/} n} \approx 1 \text{/} n . )$$
Answer: Diverges by limit comparison with $$1 \text{/} n .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Does $$\sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} \frac{1}{( \text{ln} n )^{p}}$$ converge if $$p$$ is large enough? If so, for which $$p \text{?}$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Does $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} ( \frac{( \text{ln} n )}{n} )^{p}$$ converge if $$p$$ is large enough? If so, for which $$p \text{?}$$
Answer: Converges for $$p > 1$$ by comparison with a $$p$$ series for slightly smaller $$p .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: For which $$p$$ does the series $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} 2^{p n} \text{/} 3^{n}$$ converge?

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: For which $$p > 0$$ does the series $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{n^{p}}{2^{n}}$$ converge?
Answer: Converges for all $$p > 0 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: For which $$r > 0$$ does the series $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{r^{n^{2}}}{2^{n}}$$ converge?

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: For which $$r > 0$$ does the series $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{2^{n}}{r^{n^{2}}}$$ converge?
Answer: Converges for all $$r > 1 .$$ If $$r > 1$$ then $$r^{n} > 4 ,$$ say, once $$n > \text{ln} ( 2 ) \text{/} \text{ln} ( r )$$ and then the series converges by limit comparison with a geometric series with ratio $$1 \text{/} 2 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Find all values of $$p$$ and $$q$$ such that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{n^{p}}{( n \text{!} )^{q}}$$ converges.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Does $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{\text{sin}^{2} ( n r \text{/} 2 )}{n}$$ converge or diverge? Explain.
Answer: The numerator is equal to $$1$$ when $$n$$ is odd and $$0$$ when $$n$$ is even, so the series can be rewritten $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2 n + 1} ,$$ which diverges by limit comparison with the harmonic series.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Explain why, for each $$n ,$$ at least one of $$\{ | \text{sin} n | , | \text{sin} ( n + 1 ) | \text{,ddot.} , | \text{sin} n + 6 | \}$$ is larger than $$1 \text{/} 2 .$$ Use this relation to test convergence of $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{| \text{sin} n |}{\sqrt{n}} .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Suppose that $$a_{n} \geq 0$$ and $$b_{n} \geq 0$$ and that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a^{2} _{n}$$ and $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} b^{2} _{n}$$ converge. Prove that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} b_{n}$$ converges and $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} b_{n} \le \frac{1}{2} ( \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}^{2} + \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} b_{n}^{2} ) .$$
Answer: $$( a - b )^{2} = a^{2} - 2 a b + b^{2}$$ or $$a^{2} + b^{2} \geq 2 a b ,$$ so convergence follows from comparison of $$2 a_{n} b_{n}$$ with $$a^{2} _{n}  + b^{2} _{n}  .$$ Since the partial sums on the left are bounded by those on the right, the inequality holds for the infinite series.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Does $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} 2^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} \text{ln} n}$$ converge? ( Hint: Write $$2^{\text{ln} \text{ln} n}$$ as a power of $$\text{ln} n . )$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Does $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} ( \text{ln} n )^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} n}$$ converge? ( Hint: Use $$n = e^{\text{ln} ( n )}$$ to compare to a $$p - \text{series} \text{.} )$$
Answer: $$( \text{ln} n )^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} n} = e^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} ( n ) \text{ln} \text{ln} ( n )} .$$ If $$n$$ is sufficiently large, then $$\text{ln} \text{ln} n > 2 ,$$ so $$( \text{ln} n )^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} n} < 1 \text{/} n^{2} ,$$ and the series converges by comparison to a $$p - \text{series} \text{.}$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Does $$\sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} ( \text{ln} n )^{\text{unicode[:-]} \text{ln} \text{ln} n}$$ converge? ( Hint: Compare $$a_{n}$$ to $$1 \text{/} n . )$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Show that if $$a_{n} \geq 0$$ and $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ converges, then $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a^{2} _{n}$$ converges. If $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a^{2} _{n}$$ converges, does $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ necessarily converge?
Answer: $$a_{n} \vec{} 0 ,$$ so $$a^{2} _{n} \le | a_{n} |$$ for large $$n .$$ Convergence follows from limit comparison. $$\sum 1 \text{/} n^{2}$$ converges, but $$\sum 1 \text{/} n$$ does not, so the fact that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a^{2} _{n}$$ converges does not imply that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ converges.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Suppose that $$a_{n} > 0$$ for all $$n$$ and that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ converges. Suppose that $$b_{n}$$ is an arbitrary sequence of zeros and ones. Does $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} b_{n}$$ necessarily converge?

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Suppose that $$a_{n} > 0$$ for all $$n$$ and that $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ diverges. Suppose that $$b_{n}$$ is an arbitrary sequence of zeros and ones with infinitely many terms equal to one. Does $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n} b_{n}$$ necessarily diverge?
Answer: No. $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} 1 \text{/} n$$ diverges. Let $$b_{k} = 0$$ unless $$k = n^{2}$$ for some $$n .$$ Then $$\sum_{k} b_{k} \text{/} k = \sum 1 \text{/} k^{2}$$ converges.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Complete the details of the following argument: If $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}$$ converges to a finite sum $$s ,$$ then $$\frac{1}{2} s = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{6} + \text{cdots}$$ and $$s - \frac{1}{2} s = 1 + \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} + \text{cdots} .$$ Why does this lead to a contradiction?

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Show that if $$a_{n} \geq 0$$ and $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a^{2} _{n}$$ converges, then $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \text{sin}^{2} ( a_{n} )$$ converges.
Answer: $$| \text{sin} t | \le | t | ,$$ so the result follows from the comparison test.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Suppose that $$a_{n} \text{/} b_{n} \vec{} 0$$ in the comparison test, where $$a_{n} \geq 0$$ and $$b_{n} \geq 0.$$ Prove that if $$\sum b_{n}$$ converges, then $$\sum a_{n}$$ converges.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Let $$b_{n}$$ be an infinite sequence of zeros and ones. What is the largest possible value of $$x = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} b_{n} \text{/} 2^{n} \text{?}$$
Answer: By the comparison test, $$x = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} b_{n} \text{/} 2^{n} \le \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} 1 \text{/} 2^{n} = 1.$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Let $$d_{n}$$ be an infinite sequence of digits, meaning $$d_{n}$$ takes values in $$\{ 0 , 1 \text{,unicode[:ddot.]} , 9 \} .$$ What is the largest possible value of $$x = \sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} d_{n} \text{/} 10^{n}$$ that converges?

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Explain why, if $$x > 1 \text{/} 2 ,$$ then $$x$$ cannot be written $$x = \sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} \frac{b_{n}}{2^{n}} ( b_{n} = 0 \text{or} 1 , b_{1} = 0 ) .$$
Answer: If $$b_{1} = 0 ,$$ then, by comparison, $$x \le \sum_{n = 2}^{\infty} 1 \text{/} 2^{n} = 1 \text{/} 2 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: [T] Evelyn has a perfect balancing scale, an unlimited number of $$1 \text{-kg}$$ weights, and one each of $$1 \text{/} 2 \text{-kg} , 1 \text{/} 4 \text{-kg} , 1 \text{/} 8 \text{-kg} ,$$ and so on weights. She wishes to weigh a meteorite of unspecified origin to arbitrary precision. Assuming the scale is big enough, can she do it? What does this have to do with infinite series?

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: [T] Robert wants to know his body mass to arbitrary precision. He has a big balancing scale that works perfectly, an unlimited collection of $$1 \text{-kg}$$ weights, and nine each of $$0.1 \text{-kg,}$$ $$0.01 \text{-kg} , 0.001 \text{-kg,}$$ and so on weights. Assuming the scale is big enough, can he do this? What does this have to do with infinite series?
Answer: Yes. Keep adding $$1 \text{-kg}$$ weights until the balance tips to the side with the weights. If it balances perfectly, with Robert standing on the other side, stop. Otherwise, remove one of the $$1 \text{-kg}$$ weights, and add $$0.1 \text{-kg}$$ weights one at a time. If it balances after adding some of these, stop. Otherwise if it tips to the weights, remove the last $$0.1 \text{-kg}$$ weight. Start adding $$0.01 \text{-kg}$$ weights. If it balances, stop. If it tips to the side with the weights, remove the last $$0.01 \text{-kg}$$ weight that was added. Continue in this way for the $$0.001 \text{-kg}$$ weights, and so on. After a finite number of steps, one has a finite series of the form $$A + \sum_{n = 1}^{N} s_{n} \text{/} 10^{n}$$ where $$A$$ is the number of full kg weights and $$d_{n}$$ is the number of $$1 \text{/} 10^{n} \text{-kg}$$ weights that were added. If at some state this series is Robert’s exact weight, the process will stop. Otherwise it represents the $$N \text{th}$$ partial sum of an infinite series that gives Robert’s exact weight, and the error of this sum is at most $$1 \text{/} 10^{N} .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: The series $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2 n}$$ is half the harmonic series and hence diverges. It is obtained from the harmonic series by deleting all terms in which $$n$$ is odd. Let $$m > 1$$ be fixed. Show, more generally, that deleting all terms $$1 \text{/} n$$ where $$n = m k$$ for some integer $$k$$ also results in a divergent series.

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: In view of the previous exercise, it may be surprising that a subseries of the harmonic series in which about one in every five terms is deleted might converge. A depleted harmonic series is a series obtained from $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{n}$$ by removing any term $$1 \text{/} n$$ if a given digit, say $$9 ,$$ appears in the decimal expansion of $$n .$$ Argue that this depleted harmonic series converges by answering the following questions. How many whole numbers $$n$$ have $$d$$ digits? How many $$d \text{-digit}$$ whole numbers $$h ( d ) .$$ do not contain $$9$$ as one or more of their digits? What is the smallest $$d \text{-digit}$$ number $$m ( d ) \text{?}$$ Explain why the deleted harmonic series is bounded by $$\sum_{d = 1}^{\infty} \frac{h ( d )}{m ( d )} .$$ Show that $$\sum_{d = 1}^{\infty} \frac{h ( d )}{m ( d )}$$ converges.
Answer: a. $$10^{d} - 10^{d - 1} < 10^{d}$$ b. $$h ( d ) < 9^{d}$$ c. $$m ( d ) = 10^{d - 1} + 1$$ d. Group the terms in the deleted harmonic series together by number of digits. $$h ( d )$$ bounds the number of terms, and each term is at most $$1 \text{/} m ( d ) .$$ $$\sum_{d = 1}^{\infty} h ( d ) \text{/} m ( d ) \le \sum_{d = 1}^{\infty} 9^{d} \text{/} ( 10 )^{d - 1} \le 90.$$ One can actually use comparison to estimate the value to smaller than $$80 .$$ The actual value is smaller than $$23 .$$

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Suppose that a sequence of numbers $$a_{n} > 0$$ has the property that $$a_{1} = 1$$ and $$a_{n + 1} = \frac{1}{n + 1} S_{n} ,$$ where $$S_{n} = a_{1} + \text{cdots} + a_{n} .$$ Can you determine whether $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ converges? ( Hint: $$S_{n}$$ is monotone.)

Problem Statement: Use the limit comparison test to determine whether each of the following series converges or diverges.
Question: Suppose that a sequence of numbers $$a_{n} > 0$$ has the property that $$a_{1} = 1$$ and $$a_{n + 1} = \frac{1}{( n + 1 )^{2}} S_{n} ,$$ where $$S_{n} = a_{1} + \text{cdots} + a_{n} .$$ Can you determine whether $$\sum_{n = 1}^{\infty} a_{n}$$ converges? ( Hint: $$S_{2} = a_{2} + a_{1} = a_{2} + S_{1} = a_{2} + 1 = 1 + 1 \text{/} 4 = ( 1 + 1 \text{/} 4 ) S_{1} ,$$ $$S_{3} = \frac{1}{3^{2}} S_{2} + S_{2} = ( 1 + 1 \text{/} 9 ) S_{2} = ( 1 + 1 \text{/} 9 ) ( 1 + 1 \text{/} 4 ) S_{1} ,$$ etc. Look at $$\text{ln} ( S_{n} ) ,$$ and use $$\text{ln} ( 1 + t ) \le t ,$$ $$t > 0 . )$$
Answer: Continuing the hint gives $$S_{N} = ( 1 + 1 \text{/} N^{2} ) ( 1 + 1 \text{/} ( N - 1 )^{2} \text{unicode[:ddot.]} ( 1 + 1 \text{/} 4 ) ) .$$ Then $$\text{ln} ( S_{N} ) = \text{ln} ( 1 + 1 \text{/} N^{2} ) + \text{ln} ( 1 + 1 \text{/} ( N - 1 )^{2} ) + \text{cdots} + \text{ln} ( 1 + 1 \text{/} 4 ) .$$ Since $$\text{ln} ( 1 + t )$$ is bounded by a constant times $$t ,$$ when $$0 < t < 1$$ one has $$\text{ln} ( S_{N} ) \le C \sum_{n = 1}^{N} \frac{1}{n^{2}} ,$$ which converges by comparison to the p -series for $$p = 2 .$$
